content.full_text:
DARRYL R. \flOLD
ATTORNEY AT LA\^r'
3i5I AIRWAY AVENUE. SUITE M.I
COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626
t7t4t 641-4942 
FAX (7t4) 546-lO03
Sm Ifi
$ cs ;lii 'gg
Septembet 6, 1990
Deanne Stone, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 
95804-0814
Re: Interpretation of 585802(f ) Discussed on Ju1-y 24, l-990
Dear Ms. Stone:
I recently asked you by telephone whether it would be
permissible for an incumbent officeholder to use campaign funds
to pay for the cost of an annual luncheon attended by certain of
his agencyrs employees, and by the spouse or other guest of each
of those employees, where the cost of each lunch was less than
$50, and the cost of lunch for the employee and a spouse or other
guest, together, would be less than $100.
You replied by telephone on JuLy 24, L990, that the test
would be whether the expenditure was ttdirectly relatedrr to a
political, 
legislative, or qovernmental purpose as required by
Government Code 585802(f). The only provision in 585802(f)
specifically describing expenditures that are rrdirectly relatedrr
to a permitted purpose refers to gifts of less than $1OO in a
calendar year made to rran ernployee or a campaign coxunittee
worker, or to an employee of the elected officerrs agency.rr You
said that since there is no Cornmission regulation or previous
written advice providing further guidance, your verbal advice,
based on the literal 
language of S85802(f), would have to be that
use of campaign funds to pay the cost of the lunch for the
employee would be permissible, but that palrment for the cost of
tha lunch for a spouse or other guest of the employee is not
defined as I'directly related" to a pennitted purpose so would not
be permissible.
On behalf of rny client, Brad Gates, the incumbent Sheriff of
Orange County, I arn asking for written advice on this question.
I am authoriZed to make this reguest on behatf of this client.


Deanne Stone, Esq,
September 5, l-99 o
Paqe Thro
I am also urgj.ng very strongly that, in providing written
advice, the commission reconsider the advice that you gave me
verbally, based on the literal, Language of 585802(f) alone, and
that the Cornmission advise me that the cost of the lunch for the
spouse or other guest of an employee of an officeholder's agency
would be considered t'directly relatedtr to a pennitted purpose
where the spouse or other guest r{as invited at the option of the
empl,oyee, attended e/ith the employee, and the combined cost was
less than $100. f betieve that this interpretation is the
appropriate interpretation of 585802(f), and that there are sound
reasons for this interpretation.
Permitting an employee to invite a spouse or guest to attend
an event with that enployee, at the employeers option, should be
considered a gift to the employee, and therefore be ttdirectly
relatedir to a perrnitted purpose under the specific provision of
S85802(f) (2) permitting gifts to employees of an elected
officer's agency. (By the same token, the total, cost of
attendance by the employee and the employeers spouse or other
guest $/ould of course have to be combined for purposes of the
rrless than $100" lini,tation.) 
It is clear from the circumstances
of this type of gift that the purpose of inviting the spouse or
other guest is for the benefit of the employee, and not for some
independent reason unrelated to the benefit to the employee. The
analysis that the cost of attendance is a gift to the ernployee is
therefore much more conpefling than the contrary analysis -- that
is, that the payment for the cost of attendance constitutes
separate gifts to the employee and to the empl-oyeers spouse or
guest .
The contrary anatysis -- that is, that a gift is deemed
given to each of the individuals who actually use it -- would
create an artificia] 
and unnecessary distinction in the type cf
gifts that could be given to an ernployee. That analysis h/ou]d,
for instance, prohibit gifts such as two tickets to a single
event (for an enployee and the employeers spouse or other guest) ,
but permit a gift of one ticket to each of tI^ro events (which
could be used by the empl-oyee only). Falling somewhere j.n
between might be a gift to a enpl-oyee for the enpl-oyeers hone,
where a spouse rnight also enjoy it, or a gift such as a basket of
fruit and wine, clearly intended for the eni oyrnent of two
persons, even though technically it could be consuned in its
entirety by one self-indulgent individual . (This analysis woul-d,
of course, also apply to gifts to persons other than employees,
such as to an officehol-derrs political supporters. The
officeholder could give one ticket to an event to a campaign
contributor, but not two, unless the contributor used it only for


Deanne Stone, Esq.
September 6 r l99O
Page Three
a person who is also a contributor or for whom the gift would
have an independent ttdirectly relatedrr purpose.) These
distinctions are artificial 
and totally unnecessary to serve the
reasonable purposes of 585802(f). The analysis that I urge the
Comrnission to adopt instead would avoid hairsplitting
interpretations i.n the future, where it might be questioned
whether a gift was intended for one or tvro persons. A gift to an
individual who is an employee or campaign conmittee worker, or an
employee of the elected officerrs agency, should be considered a
gift to that individual even if it can be used or consumed only
by two persons.
Lastly, the analysis that I am urging would be consistent
with Californiars cornmunity property 1aw, which provides that a
gift to an individual is that individualrs separate property
(civil 
Code SS5107, 5108). A gift to an employee, therefore,
whether cash, a basket of fruit or wine, or t\./o tickets to an
event, or l-unch for the employee and the empl-oyeers spouse or
other guest, would be consj-dered the separate property of that
employee, and therefore should be considered to be a gift to that
employee, and not in part a gift to whoever else receives part of
the enj oyrnent of it at the invitation of the employee.
I appreciate your consideration of this request. I think
that your experience nay indicate that it is a problem that may
be encountered by a number of officeholders, so $/ou1d justify the
Commissionrs close attention.
If you have any questions, please 1et me know.
DRVkn
STO}IE.LTR
S incerely ,

gift to an individual is that individualrs separate property
(civil 
Code SS5107, 5108). A gift to an employee, therefore,
whether cash, a basket of fruit or wine, or t\./o tickets to an
event, or l-unch for the employee and the empl-oyeers spouse or
other guest, would be consj-dered the separate property of that
employee, and therefore should be considered to be a gift to that
employee, and not in part a gift to whoever else receives part of
the enj oyrnent of it at the invitation of the employee.
I appreciate your consideration of this request. I think
that your experience nay indicate that it is a problem that may

sections.question_synthetic: The attorney requests written FPPC advice on whether campaign funds can be used to pay for an employee luncheon event where each employee's meal costs less than $50 and the combined cost for an employee plus spouse/guest is less than $100, seeking a broader interpretation of Government Code §85802(f) than previously given verbally.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney seeking written advice from FPPC. No formal FPPC response or conclusion is included. The document contains the legal question and detailed arguments supporting a requested interpretation, but FPPC has not yet issued their formal written advice.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether it would be permissible for an incumbent officeholder to use campaign funds to pay for the cost of an annual luncheon attended by certain of his agency's employees, and by the spouse or other guest of each of those employees, where the cost of each lunch was less than $50, and the cost of lunch for the employee and a spouse or other guest, together, would be less than $100.

embedding.summary: Attorney Darryl Mold requests written FPPC advice on behalf of Brad Gates, Orange County Sheriff, regarding the permissibility of using campaign funds to pay for annual employee luncheons that include spouses or guests of employees, where costs are under specified limits. The attorney argues that prior verbal advice was too restrictive and urges reconsideration to allow spouse/guest meal costs as gifts to the employee under §85802(f).
